terms.name: driver_age
terms.rationale: Driver age is a fundamental risk factor in motor insurance. Young drivers (<25) and elderly drivers (>70) exhibit significantly higher claim frequencies and severities due to inexperience and declining reaction times respectively. A non-linear (spline or banded) transformation is recommended.

terms.name: vehicle_age
terms.rationale: Vehicle age affects both repair costs and safety features. Older vehicles typically have lower market values but may lack modern safety technology, influencing claim severity. Newer vehicles may have higher repair costs due to advanced components. A monotone or banded effect is expected.

terms.name: age_driving_licence
terms.rationale: Years of driving experience is a strong proxy for driving skill and risk awareness, complementary to driver age. Newly licensed drivers carry higher risk regardless of age. Correlated with driver_age but provides independent information, especially for mature new drivers.
terms.actuary_note: Moderate collinearity with driver_age should be monitored; consider VIF checks during GLM fitting.

terms.name: vehicle_value
terms.rationale: Vehicle value is a primary driver of claim severity for own-damage and comprehensive covers. Higher-value vehicles attract higher repair and replacement costs. A log transformation is typically appropriate given the skewed distribution of vehicle values.

terms.name: power_to_weight_ratio
terms.rationale: Power-to-weight ratio is a strong indicator of vehicle performance and associated driving risk. High-performance vehicles are associated with higher speeds and greater accident severity. Actuarially well-established as a risk factor in motor pricing.

terms.name: liability_exposure
terms.rationale: Liability exposure directly captures the extent of third-party liability coverage, which is a primary cost driver in motor insurance. Higher liability limits increase expected claim costs. This is a core pricing variable.

terms.name: policy_type
terms.rationale: Policy type (e.g., third-party only, third-party fire and theft, comprehensive) determines the scope of coverage and directly affects expected claims. A categorical main effect is essential as it defines the coverage structure.

terms.name: business_type
terms.rationale: Business type (e.g., private, commercial, fleet) reflects the usage pattern of the vehicle. Commercial vehicles typically have higher mileage and exposure, leading to different risk profiles. A categorical main effect is standard in motor pricing.

terms.name: payment_frequency
terms.rationale: Payment frequency (monthly vs annual) is a proxy for policyholder financial behaviour and risk profile. Monthly payers are often associated with higher lapse rates and slightly different risk characteristics. Also captures administrative cost differences.
terms.actuary_note: Regulatory scrutiny may apply in some jurisdictions if payment frequency is considered a proxy for socioeconomic status. Confirm regulatory acceptability.

terms.name: bonus_score
terms.rationale: Bonus-malus score (no-claims discount) is one of the most predictive variables in motor insurance, directly reflecting individual claims history. Higher bonus scores indicate better driving records and lower expected future claims. A strong monotone decreasing effect on premium is expected.

terms.name: fuel_type
terms.rationale: Fuel type (petrol, diesel, electric, hybrid) is associated with different vehicle performance characteristics, usage patterns, and repair costs. Electric vehicles in particular have distinct repair cost profiles. A categorical main effect is justified.

terms.name: seats
terms.rationale: Number of seats is a proxy for vehicle size and usage type. Larger vehicles may carry more passengers, affecting liability exposure. However, GBM H-statistics show zero interaction with all other features, suggesting limited incremental predictive power beyond main effect.
terms.actuary_note: All H-statistics for seats interactions are 0.0, indicating seats acts purely as a main effect with no meaningful interactions. Consider whether this variable adds sufficient lift over other vehicle characteristics already in the model.

terms.name: vehicle_brand
terms.rationale: Vehicle brand captures systematic differences in repair costs, parts availability, safety ratings, and theft attractiveness across manufacturers. A categorical main effect with potentially many levels; credibility-weighted or regularised estimates may be needed for rare brands.
terms.actuary_note: High-cardinality categorical variable. Consider grouping rare brands or using a regularised encoding to avoid overfitting in the GLM.

terms.name: municipality_type
terms.rationale: Municipality type (urban, suburban, rural) captures geographic risk differences including traffic density, theft rates, and accident frequency. Urban areas typically exhibit higher claim frequencies. A well-established territorial risk factor.

terms.name: circulation_area
terms.rationale: Circulation area provides a finer geographic segmentation than municipality type, capturing regional differences in road infrastructure, weather, and local claim patterns. Complements municipality_type as a territorial variable.
terms.actuary_note: Moderate correlation with municipality_type expected. Monitor for multicollinearity and consider whether both are needed or if one can be subsumed.

terms.name: liability_exposure:business_type
terms.rationale: Strongest interaction in the GBM. The relationship between liability exposure and premium is expected to differ materially by business type: commercial vehicles typically carry higher liability limits with different risk dynamics than private vehicles. This interaction is actuarially intuitive and commonly modelled in commercial motor pricing.

terms.name: power_to_weight_ratio:vehicle_brand
terms.rationale: The risk associated with high power-to-weight ratio varies by brand due to differences in vehicle handling, safety systems, and typical driver demographics per brand. Sports-oriented brands at high power-to-weight ratios represent compounded risk. Actuarially defensible as brand-specific performance risk.
terms.actuary_note: Ensure sufficient exposure in each brand x power-to-weight cell to support credible estimates. May require smoothing or grouping of brand levels.

terms.name: bonus_score:business_type
terms.rationale: The predictive value of bonus-malus score may differ between private and commercial use. Commercial fleets may have different claims reporting behaviours and bonus accumulation patterns, making the bonus score less or differently predictive for commercial business types.

terms.name: bonus_score:liability_exposure
terms.rationale: Policyholders with poor bonus scores (high risk) combined with high liability exposure represent a compounded risk that may not be fully captured by additive main effects alone. The interaction reflects that high-risk drivers choosing high liability limits may have different risk profiles.
terms.actuary_note: Monitor for adverse selection signal: high-risk drivers selecting high liability limits could indicate awareness of risk. Actuarially justified but worth flagging to underwriting.

terms.name: policy_type:payment_frequency
terms.rationale: The relationship between payment frequency and premium may differ by policy type. Comprehensive policyholders paying monthly may exhibit different risk and lapse behaviour compared to third-party-only monthly payers. The interaction captures differential pricing adjustments across coverage tiers.
terms.actuary_note: Ensure this interaction does not serve as a proxy for socioeconomic segmentation in jurisdictions where this is restricted.

terms.name: bonus_score:policy_type
terms.rationale: The effect of claims history (bonus score) on premium is likely to differ by policy type. For comprehensive cover, a poor bonus score has greater financial implications than for third-party only. This interaction is actuarially sound and commonly observed in practice.

terms.name: bonus_score:payment_frequency
terms.rationale: Bonus score and payment frequency may jointly capture a risk-financial behaviour segment. However, this interaction is less directly actuarially motivated than others and may partly reflect socioeconomic proxies.
terms.actuary_note: Weaker actuarial justification compared to other bonus_score interactions. This may be a data-driven signal partially reflecting socioeconomic segmentation. Regulatory review recommended before inclusion. Monitor stability across validation periods.

terms.name: vehicle_value:policy_type
terms.rationale: Vehicle value has a stronger influence on premium for comprehensive policies (which cover own damage) than for third-party only policies. This interaction is fundamental to motor pricing logic and is actuarially well-justified.

terms.name: bonus_score:municipality_type
terms.rationale: The predictive power of bonus score may vary by geographic area. In high-density urban areas, even good drivers face higher claim frequencies due to traffic conditions, potentially dampening the bonus score signal. This interaction has moderate actuarial justification.
terms.actuary_note: Plausible but secondary interaction. Validate that the interaction is stable and not driven by sparse cells in rural municipality types with extreme bonus scores.

terms.name: bonus_score:driver_age
terms.rationale: Young drivers with poor bonus scores represent a compounded high-risk segment. Conversely, elderly drivers with good bonus scores may have their age risk partially offset. The interaction between driving history and age is actuarially well-established.

terms.name: vehicle_value:bonus_score
terms.rationale: High-value vehicles owned by drivers with poor claims history represent a compounded severity and frequency risk. The interaction captures that the cost of insuring an expensive vehicle is further amplified by the driver's risk profile.

terms.name: policy_type:municipality_type
terms.rationale: Geographic risk factors interact with coverage type. Urban areas may show stronger differentiation between comprehensive and third-party premiums due to higher theft and collision rates. This interaction is actuarially defensible.

terms.name: power_to_weight_ratio:fuel_type
terms.rationale: The risk associated with high power-to-weight ratio differs by fuel type. Electric vehicles with high power-to-weight ratios have different acceleration and handling characteristics compared to petrol equivalents, and different repair cost profiles. Actuarially justified.

terms.name: vehicle_brand:liability_exposure
terms.rationale: Certain vehicle brands are associated with higher third-party liability costs due to vehicle size, performance, or typical usage patterns. The interaction between brand and liability exposure level is plausible but should be validated for cell credibility.
terms.actuary_note: Risk of sparse cells for niche brands at extreme liability exposure levels. Consider grouping brands before fitting this interaction.

terms.name: vehicle_brand:municipality_type
terms.rationale: Brand-specific risk may vary geographically due to differences in theft attractiveness, typical usage, and regional brand popularity. This interaction is plausible but may be data-driven in part.
terms.actuary_note: Moderate actuarial justification. Validate that this interaction is not primarily driven by geographic brand distribution artefacts. Ensure sufficient exposure in all cells.

terms.name: bonus_score:age_driving_licence
terms.rationale: Both bonus score and years of licence are measures of driving experience and history. Their interaction may capture nuances such as experienced drivers who have recently accumulated claims, or new licence holders with clean records. Moderate actuarial justification.
terms.actuary_note: Collinearity between bonus_score and age_driving_licence should be assessed. The interaction may be partially redundant given both main effects are included.

terms.name: payment_frequency:driver_age
terms.rationale: Young drivers are more likely to pay monthly due to cash flow constraints, and this segment may have distinct risk characteristics beyond what driver_age alone captures. The interaction has some actuarial basis but is partly a socioeconomic proxy.
terms.actuary_note: Potential proxy for socioeconomic status. Regulatory review recommended. Validate that the interaction adds genuine risk differentiation beyond the main effects.

terms.name: power_to_weight_ratio:policy_type
terms.rationale: High-performance vehicles are more likely to be comprehensively insured, and the premium loading for power-to-weight ratio may differ by coverage type. The interaction is actuarially plausible.

terms.name: fuel_type:vehicle_brand
terms.rationale: The combination of fuel type and brand captures specific vehicle model risk profiles. Electric vehicles from certain brands have very different repair cost structures. This interaction is actuarially justified given the rapid evolution of EV repair costs.
terms.actuary_note: Data sparsity may be an issue for newer EV brands. Monitor credibility of estimates for emerging brand-fuel combinations.

terms.name: policy_type:circulation_area
terms.rationale: The premium differential between policy types may vary by circulation area due to regional differences in claim patterns, theft rates, and road conditions. This interaction is actuarially defensible as a territorial-coverage interaction.

terms.name: vehicle_value:fuel_type
terms.rationale: Electric and hybrid vehicles tend to cluster at higher vehicle values, and their repair costs scale differently with value compared to combustion vehicles. The interaction captures fuel-type-specific value-to-cost relationships.

terms.name: vehicle_value:payment_frequency
terms.rationale: The relationship between vehicle value and premium may differ by payment frequency. However, this interaction is less directly actuarially motivated and may reflect a socioeconomic segmentation artefact.
terms.actuary_note: Spurious or proxy interaction. High-value vehicle owners are less likely to pay monthly, creating a correlation that may not represent genuine risk differentiation. Excluded on regulatory defensibility grounds.

terms.name: power_to_weight_ratio:liability_exposure
terms.rationale: High-performance vehicles with high liability exposure represent a compounded third-party risk. Drivers of powerful vehicles may select higher liability limits, and the interaction captures the joint effect on expected liability costs.

terms.name: power_to_weight_ratio:age_driving_licence
terms.rationale: Inexperienced drivers operating high-performance vehicles represent a well-known compounded risk. This interaction is actuarially sound and commonly applied in motor pricing.

terms.name: vehicle_age:business_type
terms.rationale: Commercial vehicles may have different depreciation and maintenance patterns compared to private vehicles, altering the vehicle age risk curve. Older commercial vehicles may have higher breakdown and accident risk. Actuarially plausible.

terms.name: vehicle_brand:age_driving_licence
terms.rationale: Inexperienced drivers of certain high-performance or prestige brands represent a compounded risk. However, this interaction may partly reflect the power_to_weight_ratio:age_driving_licence interaction already captured.
terms.actuary_note: Potential redundancy with power_to_weight_ratio:age_driving_licence interaction. Assess marginal contribution after other interactions are fitted. Consider dropping if VIF or model stability is affected.

terms.name: vehicle_value:power_to_weight_ratio
terms.rationale: High-value, high-performance vehicles represent a compounded severity risk. The interaction captures that the cost implications of high power-to-weight ratio are amplified for expensive vehicles.

terms.name: liability_exposure:age_driving_licence
terms.rationale: Inexperienced drivers with high liability exposure represent a compounded third-party risk. The interaction is actuarially justified as inexperience amplifies the expected cost of high liability limits.

terms.name: circulation_area:municipality_type
terms.rationale: Both variables are territorial risk factors. Their interaction may capture finer geographic risk segmentation. However, these two variables are likely correlated, and the interaction may be partially redundant.
terms.actuary_note: Likely collinear territorial variables. The interaction risks overfitting geographic risk segmentation and may not be independently defensible. Recommend assessing whether one variable subsumes the other before including this interaction.

terms.name: liability_exposure:municipality_type
terms.rationale: The cost of liability exposure may vary by geographic area due to differences in third-party claim costs, legal environments, and accident rates. Urban areas typically have higher third-party claim costs, amplifying the effect of high liability limits.

terms.name: bonus_score:fuel_type
terms.rationale: The predictive power of bonus score may differ by fuel type, particularly for electric vehicle owners who may represent a self-selected lower-risk population. However, this interaction is at the margin of statistical and actuarial significance.
terms.actuary_note: Borderline H-statistic (0.102). The actuarial justification is weak and this interaction may be data-driven. Excluded to maintain model parsimony and regulatory defensibility.

terms.name: vehicle_value:driver_age
terms.rationale: Young drivers of high-value vehicles represent a compounded risk. The interaction captures that vehicle value risk is amplified for inexperienced or young drivers.
terms.actuary_note: Borderline H-statistic. Validate statistical significance and stability. May be partially captured by bonus_score:driver_age and vehicle_value:bonus_score interactions already included.

formula: total_premium ~ driver_age + vehicle_age + age_driving_licence + vehicle_value + power_to_weight_ratio + liability_exposure + policy_type + business_type + payment_frequency + bonus_score + fuel_type + seats + vehicle_brand + municipality_type + circulation_area + liability_exposure:business_type + power_to_weight_ratio:vehicle_brand + bonus_score:business_type + bonus_score:liability_exposure + policy_type:payment_frequency + bonus_score:policy_type + bonus_score:payment_frequency + vehicle_value:policy_type + bonus_score:municipality_type + bonus_score:driver_age + vehicle_value:bonus_score + policy_type:municipality_type + power_to_weight_ratio:fuel_type + vehicle_brand:liability_exposure + vehicle_brand:municipality_type + bonus_score:age_driving_licence + payment_frequency:driver_age + power_to_weight_ratio:policy_type + fuel_type:vehicle_brand + policy_type:circulation_area + vehicle_value:fuel_type + vehicle_value:payment_frequency + power_to_weight_ratio:liability_exposure + power_to_weight_ratio:age_driving_licence + vehicle_age:business_type + vehicle_brand:age_driving_licence + vehicle_value:power_to_weight_ratio + liability_exposure:age_driving_licence + circulation_area:municipality_type + liability_exposure:municipality_type + bonus_score:fuel_type + vehicle_value:driver_age
